forecasts.reasoning: The climate.mil portal was taken offline by the Trump administration shortly after taking office in January 2025, alongside widespread removals of federal climate data. While the USDA recently committed to restoring some of its own purged climate webpages in response to a lawsuit from farmers (who could demonstrate direct standing and reliance on the data), there is no evidence of a similar lawsuit to compel the Department of Defense to restore the climate.mil domain. Furthermore, members of the public are unlikely to have standing to sue for the restoration of a military planning portal. Since the site remains down as of early May 2025 and is controlled strictly by the DoD (a .mil domain), it will only come back online if the administration voluntarily reverses its own policy—which is extremely contrary to their documented agenda—or is forced to by a fast-moving court order, which hasn't even been filed yet. It's highly improbable the site will return by July 1, 2025.

In April 2025, HHS Secretary RFK Jr. explicitly directed the CDC to stop recommending the addition of fluoride to community drinking water. The CDC responded by stating it would re-examine the evidence, and as of May 2025, a task force was being assembled to review the policy and issue new recommendations. Given RFK Jr.'s strong, long-standing opposition to water fluoridation and his direct authority over the CDC, it is highly probable that the CDC will comply quickly. Even if the task force has not finalized a new permanent standard by July 1, 2025, standard administrative procedure for policy reversals dictates that the old recommendation (0.7 mg/L) will be suspended, removed from the active webpage, or the page will be taken down or marked "under review" in the interim. Any of these actions would resolve the question as No. The 3-month window from the April announcement to July 1 provides ample time for this straightforward website update. There remains only a small chance that bureaucratic inertia or regulatory hurdles delay the modification of the webpage past July 1.

President Trump has long opposed the Diversity Visa program (often calling it the "visa lottery"). In his first term, he tried to end it and used executive proclamations to pause its issuance (e.g., during the COVID-19 pandemic). Now in his second term (starting January 2025), reports from early 2025 indicate that his administration is exploring ways to end or severely restrict the diversity visa lottery. The prompt asks whether President Trump will issue a "presidential action" mentioning the exact words "Diversity Visa" before July 1, 2025, which must appear on the White House's presidential actions page. Given his past actions (Proclamation 10014 and 10052, etc., which explicitly mention "Diversity Visa") and current proposals to halt the program via an executive order (e.g., reports from late January/February 2025 about impending executive orders targeting diversity programs and legal immigration), it is highly likely that he will issue at least one executive order, proclamation, or memorandum that mentions the "Diversity Visa" before the deadline. While Congress created the program and must act to fully repeal it, the President has wide latitude under INA 212(f) to suspend entry, which typically requires a formal proclamation (as seen in 2020). The specific wording "Diversity Visa" is standard legal and administrative terminology. The timeframe for this question extends deeply into his first 100-plus days (up to July 1, 2025), giving ample time for an immigration executive order targeting this program to clear the administration and appear on the White House website. Therefore, I estimate a 77% probability that an executive action mentioning "Diversity Visa" will be published on the specified website.

The phrase 'snack foods' has been listed on the USDA's 'What Can SNAP Buy?' page as part of long-standing guidance reflecting the statutory definition of eligible foods under the Food and Nutrition Act. Although the Trump administration (including USDA Secretary Brooke Rollins and figures like RFK Jr.) has signaled a strong intent to restrict SNAP purchases of 'junk food' and sugary drinks, changing the actual rules requires either granting state waivers or passing congressional legislation (e.g., in a Farm Bill), which takes time. Given that the text has already survived the administration's first 100 days as of early May 2025, it is likely that bureaucratic inertia will prevail in the short term, and the webpage will not be updated until a formal policy mechanism or rule change is enacted. However, because scrubbing the phrase is an easy administrative edit that aligns with the administration’s public rhetoric, there remains a meaningful chance (~32%) that an appointee orders its removal as a quick, symbolic victory before July 1.

The resolution date is July 1, 2025, which is only about 6 weeks after the knowledge cutoff of May 20, 2025. The specific State Department URL for "Step 1 Submit an Entry" for the Diversity Visa has been stable and active for several years (at least since 2021, and likely earlier, as it is embedded in many official guides and forums). 

While President Trump has a stated desire to terminate the Diversity Visa (DV) program, doing so legally requires an act of Congress since the program was established by the Immigration Act of 1990. As of May 2025, no legislation terminating the program has passed, and sweeping immigration overhauls move slowly. Even if a restrictive executive order or legislative change occurred in the intervening 6 weeks, government websites are slow to update, and the State Department would more likely add an advisory banner to the existing page rather than deleting the established permalink immediately. The travel.state.gov domain has not undergone any recent full CMS migration that would change the standard AEM `.html` URL structures. Because the timeframe is so short and the URL architecture is highly stable, the probability of the page remaining online at the specified URL is very high.
